role_hints.oews_basis: 13-1111 Management Analysts 15.5% of wage bill + 13-1199 Business Operations Specialists 2.9% + 13-1161 Market Research Analysts 4.1%; oews_level is 4-digit 5416 proxy — this catch-all's true mix depends on the dominant sub-segment (telecom/utility cost analysts, site-selection analysts, etc.), long tail est. ~10-15% more
role_hints.note: Invoice/tariff auditing, benchmarking studies, vendor comparisons and report drafting are heavily AI-exposed; negotiation with carriers/utilities and client-side change management persist. Plausible within-role fraction 0.3-0.5.

role_hints.oews_basis: 11-1021 General and Operations Managers 13.5% + tail managers est. ~4% more
role_hints.note: 11,518 of 12,746 firms have <5 employees — this is substantially owner-operator compensation; relationships persist, coordination automates.
Tracking and reporting automate; client coordination persists.

role_hints.oews_basis: 41-3091 Sales Representatives of Services 3.6% + 43-4051 Customer Service Representatives 1.4%
role_hints.note: Outbound proposal production automates; referral-driven selling does not.
Expense-management and audit shops run internal tooling; AI-augmented.

role_hints.oews_basis: 13-2011 Accountants and Auditors 2.2% + 13-1071 HR Specialists 1.7% + office/admin long tail est. ~8-12% more
role_hints.note: Data entry, invoice processing and document handling — the core of expense-audit delivery work is itself clerical and highly automatable.

role_hints.oews_basis: not in the listed top-10; 4-digit top-10 covers ~53% of wage bill
role_hints.note: Composition depends on sub-segment — allocate after identifying the dominant one.

source_hints.source: Census Business Trends and Outlook Survey (BTOS) AI supplement
source_hints.why: Official AI-use rates by sector; the professional/technical services cut is the most defensible adoption anchor for a catch-all code with no dedicated survey.

source_hints.source: Source Global Research reports on AI and the consulting market
source_hints.why: Analyst firm covering the consulting industry specifically, including AI's effect on delivery models and pricing.
Consulting M&A deal counts and EV/EBITDA multiples spanning niche consulting segments.
SDE-basis sale multiples for the micro tier that dominates this code (90% of firms <5 employees).

source_hints.source: TEM industry coverage (AOTMP or similar telecom/technology expense management analysts)
source_hints.why: If telecom/utility expense management proves the dominant sub-segment, its contingency-fee (share-of-savings) pricing is the capture mechanism to document.

source_hints.source: Exit Planning Institute State of Owner Readiness surveys
source_hints.why: Owner-age/succession context for micro professional-services firms.

capture_questions: This code mixes fee mechanisms: expense-audit and cost-reduction work is often contingency-priced (a % of savings found — capture-friendly by construction), while advisory work bills hourly/project (deflation-prone at rescope). For the dominant sub-segment, which mechanism governs, and who keeps AI savings under it?
Who owns the client relationship — firm-level recurring audit contracts, or individual principals whose books are portable?

What stops clients from running AI directly over their own telecom/utility/vendor invoices and tariffs to find the same savings — is the moat proprietary tariff/benchmark data or just labor clients didn't want to spend?
Is revenue recurring (ongoing expense management, retained advisory) or one-shot project work — and does AI shorten the one-shot engagements it doesn't eliminate?
terminal_value_question: For the dominant sub-segment, name the mechanism that keeps paid demand alive under mature AI: if it is contingency expense-audit work, the product IS finding savings with information tools — mature AI in the client's hands replaces the product outright, not just the operator's costs; if it is negotiation/representation against carriers and utilities, human agency may persist. Which is it, and what does the evidence show clients already doing?
special_notes: CATCH-ALL CODE: 541618 is 'Other Management Consulting Services' — a residual bucket (telecommunications management consulting, utilities management consulting, expense management/audit, site selection and other niches, excluding admin/HR/marketing/logistics consulting which have their own codes). Identify the top-3 revenue sub-segments, score the DOMINANT one, and set heterogeneous: true. Best hypothesis, flagged as hypothesis: telecom/utility/technology expense management and cost-reduction consulting is the largest coherent cluster.

DATA ODDITY — treat labor_share with suspicion: the record's labor_share is 1.3991 (wages exceed receipts), which is economically impossible and signals a QCEW(2024)-vs-SUSB(2022) classification mismatch on this residual code (firms coded 541618 in one dataset and elsewhere in the other). Do not re-research the input (rule 6), but do not build V on a >1 labor share at face value — note the inconsistency, cross-check implied economics against sibling 5416 codes (labor_share 0.46-0.63), and carry the uncertainty into top_uncertain_inputs.
Pricing-model deflation candidate (advisory slice): hourly/project fees anchored to estimated effort get rescoped as AI cuts hours; contingency slice is the opposite — model pi_dist by mechanism mix.
EBITDA normalization: 90% of firms are <5 employees; profit figures are SDE with owner comp inside — normalize before applying multiples.

Boundary confusion: siblings 541611 (admin/general management), 541612 (HR), 541613 (marketing), 541614 (logistics) absorb most recognizable consulting; anything branded 'management consulting' in surveys is mostly NOT this code — verify a source's segment definition before using its numbers.
